prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 About me: I'm a 42-year-old Black man.
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
 - History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.
 - Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 72, Temp 37.0°C
 - Exam: Right-sided facial weakness affecting both the forehead and lower face. I can raise my eyebrows weakly on the right. Eye doesn't fully close. No other neurological deficits noted.
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Glucose: 94 mg/dL (70-100) normal
 - HbA1c: 5.4% (4.0-5.6) normal
 - WBC: 7.2 K/uL (4.0-11.0) normal
 - ESR: 12 mm/hr (0-22) normal
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D – choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24–48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right side of the face including the forehead, trouble closing the right eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) most strongly fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy, often called Bell’s palsy, which can follow a recent viral illness. Your labs (glucose, A1c, WBC, ESR) are normal and don’t suggest diabetes or a major infection/inflammatory condition. Even though this pattern is less typical for stroke than “forehead-sparing” facial weakness, new facial droop is still treated as urgent because stroke and other causes must be ruled out, and Bell’s palsy treatment is time-sensitive (steroids work best if started within 72 hours). Also, incomplete eye closure needs eye-protection guidance.  
TRIAGE: D  
CONFIDENCE: 70%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right side of the face including the forehead, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) most strongly fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy, often called Bell’s palsy. This can happen after a viral illness like a recent cold. Your labs are normal and don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or significant inflammation. Even though this pattern is less typical for stroke, a new facial droop should be evaluated promptly because early treatment for Bell’s palsy (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) works best when started within about 72 hours, and your eye needs protection if it can’t close fully to prevent corneal damage.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right side of the face including the forehead, inability to fully close the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit best with a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy, often called Bell’s palsy, which can follow a recent viral illness. Your labs shown are normal and don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or significant inflammation. Even if this is Bell’s palsy, treatment is time-sensitive: medicines (often steroids) work best when started within about 72 hours, and protecting the eye is important because it may dry out or get injured if it can’t close fully. Stroke is less likely with forehead involvement and no other deficits, but new facial weakness should be assessed promptly.  
TRIAGE: C  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam show weakness of the entire right side of the face (forehead and lower face) with trouble closing the right eye, and no arm/leg weakness or speech problems. This pattern most strongly fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), which can happen after a recent viral illness. Your labs are normal and don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or significant inflammation. Even if this is Bell’s palsy, timing matters: treatments like steroids are most helpful when started early (ideally within 72 hours). Also, because sudden facial weakness can rarely be a stroke mimic, a clinician should confirm the diagnosis. Protecting the eye (lubricating drops/ointment and taping at night) is important to prevent corneal injury.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

Your labs (glucose, HbA1c, WBC, ESR) are normal and don’t suggest infection, diabetes-related nerve issues, or significant inflammation. Even if this is Bell’s palsy, treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) works best when started early (ideally within 72 hours). Also, not being able to fully close the eye can lead to corneal injury, so eye protection/lubrication is important. Stroke is less likely given forehead involvement and no other deficits, but new facial weakness should be assessed promptly.  
TRIAGE: C
